title: Evaluate replacing async_trait macro with native async-fn-in-trait
description: SecretStore and SigningBackend use the async_trait macro which boxes all futures as Pin<Box<dyn Future + Send>>. This commits the public API to Send bounds on all futures, which may be too restrictive for some backends (e.g. single-threaded HSM drivers). Rust 1.75 (our MSRV) supports native async fn in trait, but that introduces a different constraint: the compiler generates unnameable associated Future types, making it hard for callers to store or compose backends without boxing. Daniel Franke flagged this in review, noting that a single Send/!Send choice usually proves insufficient over time. Options: (a) keep async_trait (simple, works, limits Send flexibility), (b) switch to native AFIT with explicit Send bounds via a where clause or trait alias, (c) expose both Send and !Send variants via a sealed trait pair. Needs research and a decision record before any code change.
design:
Decision: keep async_trait, add Send rationale to trait rustdoc. No code change to traits.

Rationale: secretx targets daemons — callers store Arc<dyn SecretStore> and await from tokio thread-pool tasks. Send is the correct bound for this use case. The async_trait box allocation is construction-time overhead only (backends are constructed once at startup). Native AFIT is not viable for Box<dyn Trait> use cases in Rust 1.75 — unnameable future types break callers that store or compose backends. Send/!Send trait pair (option c) doubles trait surface and solves a problem no backend has actually hit — HSM backends handle thread-safety internally via Arc<Mutex<...>> on the struct, not on the future.

Action: add a doc comment to SecretStore and SigningBackend explaining the Send bound and why !Send is not offered. This addresses Daniel Franke's review concern without a breaking change.

Rejected:
- Native AFIT: Box<dyn SecretStore> doesn't work without dynosaur or manual DynSecretStore wrappers — too much caller friction
- Send+!Send pair: doubles every backend impl for a theoretical future need, no concrete case today
